text: This is our single anastomosis with duodenal ileostomy and sleeve gastrectomy otherwise known as CDS presentation. Hello, I'm Dr. Daniel Cottom and we've received many requests from our patients to have a full video of our CDS also known as our lube duodenal switch procedure. performed and put out on the internet. Here we're going to, with the following video, show you how we do the looped abdominal switch in our daily practice, and this is true for both Dr. Medlin and I. I hope you enjoy the video.

The surgeon locates the ileocecal valve, where the colon meets the small bowel, and traces backward along the small intestine to identify the site for the duodenum-ileostomy connection. This site is marked by tacking it to a piece of fat for easy identification later in the procedure.

The surgeon uses a cauterizing device to take down blood vessels connecting the arteries and fat to the stomach, achieving a dry field. The stomach is dissected from top to bottom, approaching the pylorus. Filmy adhesions are removed to expose the duodenum. A window is created through the fat layer to place a stapler across the pylorus. A standard bariatric clamp is used to excise the stomach, and a GIA stapler is positioned next to the clamp to create a straight staple line, preventing sleeve strictures.

The surgeon completes the sleeve gastrectomy and uses a liver retractor to move the liver aside for better access. A GIA stapler is positioned beyond the pylorus to cut across the duodenum. Reinforcement from the stapler device is removed, followed by the tacking stitch. The proximal duodenum is then attached to the distal loop limb, identified as the ileum.

The surgeon uses absorbable sutures to connect the cut edge of the duodenum on the right to the small bowel loop limb on the left, ensuring the connection is tension-free. The sutures are placed along the cut edge, maintaining proximity to the fat layer while penetrating the small bowel.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to create openings on either side of the duodenum, forming an enterostomy without causing bleeding. The posterior layer is then sewn together using absorbable sutures, ensuring a secure connection between the duodenal halves.

The surgeon uses an endo stitch to hand sew a running stitch along the anterior portion of the duodenal opening, closing it with precision. An anti-obstruction stitch is placed to ensure food travels down the correct side of the loop channel.

The surgeon performs a second anti-obstruction stitch to ensure there is no tension on the connection points. Air is blown into the stomach and small bowel to check for leaks, with no bubbles observed, indicating a secure connection.

After completing the air leak test, the surgeon dilates the abdominal wall and uses a large clamp to extract the stomach through a small incision. The stomach's distensible nature facilitates its removal. A diagram illustrates the completed sleeve gastrectomy and the small bowel connection, characteristic of the SADI-S procedure.

Our average operative time is approximately seventy minutes and people who have this done usually stay one day in the hospital or just an overnight stay in the surgical center. Thank you for paying attention to us. Have a good day.
